turns.content: I'm onboarding to this new project and I thought using Option<T> would be the standard way to handle nullable integers, but I keep seeing code that uses core::option::NonZeroU32 everywhere instead of just wrapping a u32. Is it actually better than regular Option<u32>?

That's a common initial assumption, but there's a crucial distinction here. While Option<T> is great for representing absence of value, NonZeroU32 represents the presence of a non-zero integer. The key difference is size: if you need to store an Option<NonZeroU32>, it can be reduced to exactly 4 bytes because it eliminates the extra byte needed for the boolean flag that Option<u32> requires for its internal representation.
So if I have a value like Some(5) in Option<u32>, does that take up more space than NonZeroU32?

Exactly. An Option<u32> is always 8 bytes on 64-bit systems because it needs an extra byte for the flag plus padding, whereas a single NonZeroU32 is just 4 bytes. This isn't about safety or correctness though; these types are different primitives designed for specific use cases.
I see, so they're both safe but optimized differently? How do I actually construct them in code?

You can't just wrap a zero value with NonZeroU32 because it would violate the type invariant. You need to use core::option::NonZeroU32::new(value) where value is non-zero, or use the from_fn constructor if you're building it programmatically.
Wait, so what happens if I try to create a NonZeroU32 with zero? Does it panic?
Yes, attempting to construct a NonZeroU32 from zero will panic because that violates the type's invariant. The design ensures you never accidentally store a zero value in this type.
That makes sense for safety but what about when I need to check if something is zero? Can't I just use regular u32 and add logic?

You absolutely can, and often should in many cases. NonZero types are niche optimizations for specific performance-critical paths where you know the value will never be zero. For general logic checking if a number is non-zero, plain u32 with an != 0 check is clearer and more idiomatic.
So when should I reach for NonZeroU8 through NonZeroU64 instead of just using Option or regular integers?

Use these types when you have a concrete, non-zero integer value that you want to store in memory without the overhead of Option's internal flag. It's about eliminating unnecessary space and ensuring the data is always valid at runtime.

Why does this even work this way? I'm looking at the implementation of a non-zero integer type and it feels like magic to me. Why would we have these special wrapper types when regular integers should just be enough for most cases?

You're right, standard integers are fine for general use, but the NonZeroU8 through NonZeroU64 family exists to avoid storing unnecessary null values. When you wrap a non-zero integer in an Option<NonZeroU32>, it allows you to represent optional data using just 4 bytes instead of needing extra space for the option itself.
That sounds convenient, but I still don't get why we can't just use Option<i32> with a custom default value. It seems like this adds unnecessary complexity to our codebase just for convenience.

You're absolutely right that it adds some boilerplate, but the benefit is in how these types handle memory layout and type safety. The NonZero variants guarantee you never accidentally pass a zero value where something else was expected, which prevents subtle bugs in your systems code.
Okay, I understand the type safety benefit, but what about performance? Does this actually make things faster or just more verbose?

Performance-wise it's mostly neutral since the compiler optimizes away the extra checks in most cases. The real win is compile-time guarantees that stop you from using zero values when your logic requires non-zero ones, like in a network protocol parser where receiving zero means connection failure.
Fair point about the protocol parser use case. So if I'm building something similar to that, should I just start using these types everywhere or only when I actually need the safety guarantee?

Use them selectively where zero values are semantically invalid for your data structure. For general-purpose application logic, regular integers with explicit checks work fine, but for things like network state machines or configuration flags where 'no value' means a specific error condition, the NonZero types save you from thinking about edge cases.
We're seeing memory bloat in our binary and I want to check if using core::option Option<NonZeroU32> instead of regular integers could help us save space.

That's a great observation about the niche optimization. Think of it like packing books into boxes: with a standard integer, every byte is always filled even if the number is zero, but NonZero types are like those special compact boxes where empty spots get removed entirely, so Option<NonZeroU32> takes up only 4 bytes instead of 8 because the inner value never allows zeros. This matters when you're storing millions of IDs in your database.
I see how that saves space, but what about our existing code using Option<i32>? Can we just swap them out without breaking anything?

You'd need to be careful because the constructors and trait bounds are different; NonZeroU32 requires a non-zero value so you can't pass zero, which means your logic needs updating. The why here is that Rust's type system enforces safety at compile time by preventing accidental zeros in these optimized types, so while it saves memory, it changes how your data flows through the application.
I just finished implementing the counter logic using Option<u32> and it works fine, but I'm wondering if there's a more efficient way to store this in memory since we never actually pass null values.

You're right that you don't need the overhead of Option here. Since your counter is guaranteed to always be positive, you could use NonZeroU32 instead. This type wraps an unsigned integer but ensures it's never zero, which saves space.
That sounds good but I'm confused about how the storage actually works and why anyone would bother with this extra wrapper type when Option<u32> is so simple to use.

The key insight is that NonZeroU32 uses niche bit packing internally. When you store it, the compiler aligns the bits differently than a plain u32 because it knows the value can't be zero. This means Option<NonZeroU32> actually takes only 4 bytes total instead of 8 bytes if you were storing an optional u64.

Wow, that's interesting but I still don't see the practical benefit over just using a regular u32 with some manual checks before operations since we're in a startup environment where performance matters more than academic purity.

The real advantage is safety at compile time without runtime overhead. With NonZeroU32 you can't accidentally pass zero to functions that require positive integers, and the compiler enforces this through the trait impl Serialize for it which prevents invalid states from ever reaching your code.

So basically I'm saying we should just use NonZeroU32 in our counter implementation since we're already using serde_json::Value for serialization anyway and this would make it cleaner without needing any runtime validation?

Exactly. By switching to core::option::Option<core::num::NonZeroU32> you get both the safety guarantees and memory efficiency while keeping your existing serialization pipeline intact since NonZero types implement standard traits just like regular integers.
